_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to develop and use historical questions that guide our investigations and help us build well-structured arguments.

_source.w: We are learning to develop and use historical questions that guide our investigations and help us build well-structured arguments.
y: Strong questions are the engine of historical inquiry — they determine the quality of our investigation.
Learning to craft analytical questions helps us move beyond description to explanation and evaluation.
This skill is foundational for academic work and for being a critically engaged citizen.
eg: Developing a question that focuses on causes, consequences, significance, or perspectives.
Using sub-questions to break a complex investigation into manageable parts.
Refining our questions based on initial research — narrowing or redirecting as needed.
Testing our questions — are they researchable, specific, and analytically productive?
Comparing questions with peers and collaborating to strengthen them.
Using our questions to structure a research plan with clear steps.
